learning_outcomes.description: Physics Theory and Concepts - explain key physics concepts, and using mathematical techniques, apply physics theory and unifying laws relating to electricity, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, and relativity.

Physics Theory and Concepts - demonstrate an understanding of scientific methods, and the critical role of integrity and ethics in scientific progress, including the ability to: (a) Propose scientific questions/hypotheses and develop theoretical models that make testable predictions relating to these questions/hypotheses; (b) Develop, perform, and extract meaningful measurements from experiments to test predictions of scientific models; (c) Evaluate the validity of theoretical predictions and experimental measurements, through application of critical reasoning.

Physics Theory and Concepts - articulate scientific processes both verbally and in writing in a style appropriate to the discipline; including explanations of: (a) Scientific questions/hypotheses; (b) Development and evaluation of theoretical models; (c)	Critical reasoning, experimental procedures and conclusions; (d) Communication of experimental measurement outcomes.

assessments.assessment_title: Active Learning Engagement - reading quizzes and workshop concept quizzes - formative assessment
assessments.due_date:
27/07/2026 - 30/10/2026

Each reading quiz is due 5pm the day before the corresponding workshop. Each workshop concept quiz is completed during the workshop. There are no late submissions or extensions.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment item is designed to encourage preparation for workshops and active participation during class. It consists of two components: reading quizzes and workshop concept quizzes.

Reading quizzes: Before each workshop, you will be assigned textbook reading to help prepare you for the learning activities. After completing the reading, you should attempt the associated reading quiz. Each reading quiz contains:

  • A content-related question that must be answered to receive credit for the quiz.
  • An optional reflection question asking what you found difficult, confusing, or interesting in the reading.

Your responses will be used by teaching staff to identify topics that require additional explanation during workshops. The quizzes are assessed on participation and effort rather than correctness.

Workshop concept quizzes: During workshops, you will answer multiple-choice concept questions using the EchoPoll system. To receive credit for a workshop concept quiz, you must submit responses to at least 75% of the concept questions asked during that workshop. These quizzes are intended to support learning and provide immediate feedback on your understanding of the material.

Grade calculation: There will be 22 reading quizzes and 22 workshop concept quizzes during the semester, giving a total of 44 opportunities to earn participation credit.

For each reading quiz and workshop concept quiz completed successfully, you will earn one participation credit.

Your grade for this assessment item will be calculated as:

Grade = 7 × minimum(Total participation credits/16,1)

where:

Total participation credits = Reading quiz credits + Workshop concept quiz credits

You therefore need to earn 16 participation credits to receive a grade of 7 for this assessment item.

Because there are substantially more opportunities to earn full marks (44 opportunities for 16 required credits), extensions and deferred assessment are not available for reading quizzes or workshop concept quizzes.

Students are nevertheless strongly encouraged to complete as many quizzes as possible throughout the semester, as these activities are designed to support learning and improve success in the course.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of these activities are not possible as students need to complete the quizzes either before or during the workshop. There are 22 content workshops during semester, giving a maximum of 44 possible marks for this assessment item. As only 16 are required for full marks, no late submissions are accepted.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
MasteringPhysics is the online homework platform that accompanies the prescribed textbook for this course. The assigned problems are designed to help you learn the course material and develop the skills and understanding required to achieve the learning objectives of the course.

Questions will be assigned for each week of course content and are due on the Monday following your Applied Class for that topic. We strongly recommend completing the assigned problems soon after the material is covered in workshops. This will help consolidate your understanding and keep you on track with the course, as many concepts build on material introduced earlier in the semester.

You are encouraged to attempt the MasteringPhysics problems before attending your Applied Class, where your instructor will provide assistance with problem solving and answer questions. You may also ask general questions on the course discussion board.

As you work through the problems, you are encouraged to clearly write out your reasoning and solution method on paper rather than simply entering answers into the online system. Seeking feedback on your written solutions during Applied Classes will help you develop effective problem-solving habits and prepare you for the End of Semester examination.

Artificial intelligence tools can provide useful support when used appropriately. However, relying on AI-generated solutions without attempting the problems yourself is unlikely to help you develop the understanding and problem-solving skills needed for success in the End of Semester examination. Instead, we encourage you to use AI as a learning aid—for example, by asking for hints, explanations of concepts, or feedback on your reasoning—while still working through the solutions yourself.

We also encourage you to discuss problem-solving approaches with your classmates. Collaborative learning can be a highly effective way to deepen your understanding and develop confidence in applying physics concepts.
assessments.submission_guidelines: From Blackboard access the link for "MasteringPhysics" from Pearson. There you will find the problems for each topic and you answer them online. You can do these at your own pace throughout the semester.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
The pre-practical work must be completed before the practical session, and checked off at the start.

All remaining practical checkpoints must be checked off before the end of your practical session.

Your practical worksheet must be submitted within 24 hours of completing the practical.

The post-practical assessment must be submitted within one week of the end of the practical session you attended.
assessments.category: Notebook/ Logbook, Quiz, Reflection, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
There are five practical sessions in PHYS1002 during the semester. These take place every two weeks between Week 3 and Week 12, depending on your allocated practical group. Each practical is a three-hour session in which you will carry out an experiment, complete a worksheet, and answer questions about the investigation. Each practical includes a series of checkpoints that you must successfully complete.

Before each practical: You must complete a pre-practical quiz on Blackboard to demonstrate that you have prepared for the practical. This quiz forms the first checkpoint for the practical session. You must answer every question correctly to pass the quiz; however, unlimited attempts are available. Students who have not satisfactorily completed the pre-practical quiz will not be permitted to enter the laboratory.

During the practical: At various checkpoints throughout the session, your demonstrator will review your worksheet and confirm that you are making satisfactory progress. To pass each checkpoint, you must maintain an appropriate record of your work in the worksheet, participate diligently and collaboratively in the practical activities, and remain in the laboratory until your demonstrator has confirmed that the checkpoint requirements have been met.

After the practical: You must upload your completed worksheet via Blackboard within 24 hours of your practical session. The worksheet itself will not be formally marked, as feedback on your work will be provided by demonstrators during the practical checkpoints.

Post-practical assessment: The post-practical assessment is a written assessment completed outside of class. It contains a number of questions based on the knowledge, skills, and data obtained during the practical session. Marks for this assessment item are awarded through the post-practical assessments.

The post-practical assessment is intended to be completed after you have completed the pre-practical quiz, attended the practical session, satisfied all practical checkpoints, and uploaded your worksheet. It is not a substitute for attendance at the practical, and students who do not complete the required practical activities and checkpoints will not receive marks for the corresponding post-practical assessment.

You must submit completed post-practical assessment via the link on Blackboard within one week of completing the practical. The assessment will then be marked by your demonstrator.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The checkpoints will be marked in your practical session.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
During Practical 3, you will perform an experiment that forms the basis of a laboratory report. Following the return of feedback and marks for the associated post-practical assessment, you will prepare and submit a more formal laboratory report based on this experiment.

To support you in this task, you will be able to book an individual consultation with teaching staff in Weeks 11 and 12 to receive feedback on a draft of your report.

Full details of the report, including assessment criteria, submission requirements, and consultation booking arrangements, will be provided on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Your laboratory report will be prepared as a pdf document and will be submitted via the link on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The examination will be invigilated on-campus for all students.

 The standard PHYS1002 formula sheet will be supplied for your convenience.

The exam will cover the content from the whole semester (Modules 1, 2, 3). Examples of recent exams are available on the UQ Library web-site. Written solutions for these exams are not available. Selected exam style questions will be worked through in workshops and applied classes.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Textbook reading and workshop preparation

Engaging with the textbook is the foundation for all that follows in this course. Studies have shown that even the best content-focussed lectures are a poor substitute for simply reading the text. You will therefore be asked to read through relevant sections of the text before each class to make yourself familiar with the concepts that will be covered in the workshops.
learning_activities.topic_description: Textbook reading and workshop preparation Engaging with the textbook is the foundation for all that follows in this course. Studies have shown that even the best content-focussed lectures are a poor substitute for simply reading the text. You will therefore be asked to read through relevant sections of the text before each class to make yourself familiar with the concepts that will be covered in the workshops.

learning_activities.topic:
Workshops

Workshops will assume a basic familiarity with the content from assigned textbook readings. (Replicating what you could read for yourself in the text is a waste of valuable class time.) Instead in workshops we focus on the much more difficult task of processing the content: working through conceptual issues, exploring the implications of the theory, and developing strategies for solving problems. Workshops will be held on campus, and will make use of interactive features such as real-time polling and questionnaires.

learning_activities.topic:
Applied Classes and MasteringPhysics

To succeed in this course you will need to keep up regular practice on problem solving. Casual academics will provide guidance and feedback as you work through example problems. They can also provide assistance with MasteringPhysics exercises. Applied Classes will be conducted on campus. MasteringPhysics provides a platform to practice solving problems related directly to the textbook material. It provides hints and immediate feedback to support your learning.

Casual academics will provide guidance and feedback as you work through example problems. They can also provide assistance with MasteringPhysics exercises. Applied Classes will be conducted on campus. MasteringPhysics provides a platform to practice solving problems related directly to the textbook material. It provides hints and immediate feedback to support your learning.

learning_activities.topic:
Practical Laboratories

In practical classes you will design and perform experiments to deepen your understanding of relevant physics concepts and to develop your scientific thinking and communication skills.
